coredata.dc:description: The preparation of functional long-wavelength-emitting nanomaterials and the researches on their applications in antibacterial and antibiofilm fields have important significance. This paper reports the preparation of yellow-green-fluorescent and high- quantum yield carbon dots (4-ACDs) with 4-aminosalicylic acid and polyethylene imine as raw materials through one-step route, and the impacts of raw material structure and the reaction conditions upon the optical properties of the products have been investigated. 4-ACDs exhibit excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, and their good biocompatibility ensures them as ideal fluorescent nano-probe for cell imaging. However, 4-ACDs could not effectively eliminate the biofilm of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). CDs-LZM complex was prepared through the coupling between 4-ACDs and lysozyme (LZM) and the complex showed strong antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria, particularly with MIC against S. aureus at 5 μg mL−1. Besides, CDs-LZM showed excellent ability against the biofilm of S. aureus. At the concentration of 60 μg mL−1, its inhibition rate against the growth of biofilm was 86 %, and elimination rate against biofilm reached 76 %. CDs-LZM exhibited obvious antibiofilm ability through removing extracellular matrix of biofilm, greatly reducing the thickness of biofilm under confocal microscopy. The application of novel long-wavelength-emitting nanomaterial in eliminating pathogenic bacteria is of great significance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The bar graphs of the PL intensity of 4-ACDs as a function of (a) pH, (b) temperature and (c) time in synthesis. The excitation wavelength of all tests was 390 nm.

(a) TEM image of the 4-ACDs. The inserts are HRTEM images (right) and the particle size distribution of 4-ACDs (left). The scale is 10 nm. (b) UV–Vis spectra and emission spectra of 4-ACDs. The inset is under ambient lighting (top) and 365 nm UV light (bottom). (c) Emission spectra of 4-ACDs excited from 350 nm to 450 nm. (d) PL spectra of 4-ACDs as pH values increased from 2 to 13 at 390 nm excitation. Inset displays 4-ACDs in phosphate buffer with pH values ranging from 2 to 13 under UV light of 365 nm.
(a) TEM image and (b) HRTEM image of the CDs-LZM. The scale is 10 nm. (c) The particle size distribution histogram of CDs-LZM.

(a) UV–Vis spectra, (b) FT-IR spectra, (c) Zeta potential of LZM, 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM. The inset in (a) shows the emission spectra of CDs-LZM, under ambient lighting (top) and 365 nm UV light (bottom).
SEM images of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa treated (a, d) without or with (b, e) 4-ACDs, (c, f) CDs-LZM for 6 h, respectively. The scales are 2 μm.
The CD spectra of (a) DNA and (b) protein of S. aureus treated without and with the 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM, respectively.

Cell viability of J774A.1 macrophages incubated with various concentrations of 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM. Data are expressed as mean ± SD of triplicate independent experiments. Different letters in the same index indicate significant differences with P < 0.05.
CLSM images after incubation S. aureus with 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM at bright field (a, e), excitation wavelengths, (b, f) 493 nm (green), (c, g) 557 nm (red), and overlap (d, h). The scales are 10 μm.

The growth curves of S. aureus biofilms treated with different concentrations of (a) 4-ACDs, (b) LZM and (c) CDs-LZM at different times within 48 h were measured at OD600. (d) Inhibition rate of 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM on S. aureus at 12 h, 24 h, 36 h and 48 h. Data are expressed as mean ± SD of triplicate independent experiments. Different letters in the same index indicate significant differences with P < 0.05.

The elimination rates of (a) 4-ACDs, (b) LZM and (c) CDs-LZM on mature S. aureus biofilms. (d) Orifice plate diagram after crystal violet staining biofilms of untreated and treated with 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM. Data are expressed as mean ± SD of triplicate independent experiments. Different letters in the same index indicate significant differences with P < 0.05.

The S. aureus biofilms metabolic activities and viability in the absence and presence different concentrations of (a) 4-ACDs, (b) LZM and (c) CDs-LZM were measured at OD600. Data are expressed as mean ± SD of triplicate independent experiments. Different letters in the same index indicate significant differences with P < 0.05.

The influences without and with different concentrations of 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM on (a) Polysaccharide, (b) protein and (c) nucleic acid were measured at 490 nm, 562 nm and 595 nm respectively. (d) Reduction rate in polysaccharide, protein and nucleic acid content being present within the EPS of S. aureus with different concentrations of 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM. Data are expressed as mean ± SD of triplicate independent experiments. Different letters in the same index indicate significant differences with P < 0.05.
CLSM images show the 2D-view and 3D-view of anti-biofilm potential of S. aureus with 4-ACDs (a, b) and CDs-LZM (c, d) at 493 nm, respectively. The scales are 10 μm.
The preparations and PL spectra of SA-CDs, 3-ACDs, 4-ACDs, and 5-ACDs, respectively. The inset displays the photographs under ambient lighting (top) and 365 nm UV light (bottom).
MICs of 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM on different microbes.

head.ce:title: Preparations of antibacterial yellow-green-fluorescent carbon dots and carbon dots-lysozyme complex and their applications in bacterial imaging and bacteria/biofilm inhibition/clearance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation of functional long-wavelength-emitting nanomaterials and the researches on their applications in antibacterial and antibiofilm fields have important significance. This paper reports the preparation of yellow-green-fluorescent and high- quantum yield carbon dots (4-ACDs) with 4-aminosalicylic acid and polyethylene imine as raw materials through one-step route, and the impacts of raw material structure and the reaction conditions upon the optical properties of the products have been investigated. 4-ACDs exhibit excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, and their good biocompatibility ensures them as ideal fluorescent nano-probe for cell imaging. However, 4-ACDs could not effectively eliminate the biofilm of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). CDs-LZM complex was prepared through the coupling between 4-ACDs and lysozyme (LZM) and the complex showed strong antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria, particularly with MIC against S. aureus at 5 μg mL−1. Besides, CDs-LZM showed excellent ability against the biofilm of S. aureus. At the concentration of 60 μg mL−1, its inhibition rate against the growth of biofilm was 86 %, and elimination rate against biofilm reached 76 %. CDs-LZM exhibited obvious antibiofilm ability through removing extracellular matrix of biofilm, greatly reducing the thickness of biofilm under confocal microscopy. The application of novel long-wavelength-emitting nanomaterial in eliminating pathogenic bacteria is of great significance.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel type of photoluminescence (PL) spherical carbon-based nano-material with super-small nano-scaled size (<10 nm), excellent water-solubility, low toxicity, easy preparation and functionalization process, making them attractive in many fields [1–3]. In recent years, the researches of CDs as an ideal environment-friendly antibacterial nanomaterial have raised great interests from researchers. For instance, Zhao et al. prepared quaternized carbon quantum dots (qCQDs) with broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and effective therapeutic effect on wounds infected by mixed bacteria [4]. Although CDs have made great achievements in antibacterial fields, most prepared antibacterial CDs exhibited short emission wavelength with blue fluorescence, which has weak tissue penetration ability and easy absorption by biological tissues, greatly limiting their applications in cell imaging [5], and thus their applications in dynamic tracking of pathogenic bacteria. Besides, the quantum yield (QY) of CDs is also another critical factor for biological imaging [6,7]. Therefore, designing efficient antibacterial CDs with long-wavelength emitting and high QY is quite challenging.

Biofilms are defined as the microbial community, and their cells are surrounded by self-produced and metabolized matrix of extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) which is mainly composed of polysaccharides, proteins and other biomolecules [8]. Compared with free planktonic bacteria, this complicated surface structure could work as the stubborn barrier to protect bacteria, making them more difficult to be eliminated. For the present, CDs have exhibited excellent ability in inhibiting Gram-positive (G+) and Gram-negative (G−) bacteria [9–11], but their ability is pretty weak for bacteria under the protection of biofilm. In addition, the formation of biofilm is regarded as the major reason for chronic infection [12]. With nano-size, high surface/volume ratio [13], the generation of structural defects [14], and stronger interaction and permeability in the biofilm matrix than drug molecules, CDs are conducive to better destruction of the biofilm architecture. Therefore, the development of antibacterial nano-materials that could effectively inhibit and eliminate biofilm has attracted much attention from the researchers.

The rich presence of functional groups on the surface of CDs endows great potential in combination with other materials, breaking the limits of single material and expanding their biochemical applications, especially in antibacterial and antibiofilm fields. Recent studies showed that the coupling of CDs with other biochemical active substances, such as antibiotics [15–17], heavy metal nanoparticles [18–20], antibacterial peptides [21], proteases [22,23] and DNA [24], etc., could enhance the antibacterial and antibiofilm ability of CDs. However, most of the coupled substances in the studies were antibiotics and heavy metal nanoparticles, which could lead to drug resistance or environmental toxicity. Therefore, researchers turned their interests to other active materials that possess excellent biocompatibility and antibacterial abilities. For example, Yu et al. prepared nanocomposite ZIF-8@Lys-CD@ FA by co-encapsulation of antibacterial ligands (lysine carbon dots, Lys CDs) and targeting drugs (folic acid, FA) with zeolite imidazole ester skeleton 8 (ZIF-8), and the composite produces highly active hydroxyl radical in acidic environment, and effectively destroys mature biofilm [25]. Lysozyme (LZM) is a natural enzyme antibacterial agent with good biocompatibility and high-efficient specificity, but its protein characteristics greatly limit its practical applications and promote the researches on functionalization and modification of LZM [26,27]. For the present, researchers have coupled LZM with gold nanoparticles [28,29], chitosan nanoparticles [30], nano-fibers [31], etc. The complexes have been applied in the delivery of biomolecules, antibacterial, sensing and other fields [32]. However, the studies on the coupling between CDs and LZM are less investigated [33], and those on their ability in eliminating biofilm are even rarer. The development of long-wavelength emitting CDs and their combination with LZM, and further studies of the application and mechanism of antibacterial/biofilm complex, are of great significance in biomedical fields.

This article reports the synthesis of high-QY yellow-green emitting 4-ACDs through hydrothermal treatment of polyethylene imine and 4-aminosalicylic acid, and the product has been characterized by various means. The prepared 4-ACDs exhibit excellent antibacterial ability, but their ability in inhibiting and eliminating biofilm is quite unsatisfactory. Therefore, the highly effective antibiofilm CDs-LZM complex was developed to realize the growth-inhibition and elimination of biofilm of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). The inhibition ability of CDs-LZM against the formation of biofilm (48 h), the elimination rate of mature biofilm, the cell survival rate inside biofilm, the impacts upon the micromorphology and EPS have been investigated to assess the inhibition ability and mechanism against the biofilm. Owing to the excellent fluorescent properties of 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM, they could be further applied in bacteria imaging to realize the monitoring of bacteria.

Salicylic acid (SA) was acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 3-aminosalicylic acid (3-ASA) and 5-aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA) were purchased from Shanghai Rhawn Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. 4-aminosalicylic acid (4-ASA) was provided by Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Ethyleneimine polymer (MW.600, PEI) was bought from Shanghai Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Egg white lysozyme was purchased by Sigma Chemical Co., Ltd. Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments. Other materials used in the experiment and instruments used for characterization are included in the supplementary materials.

4-ACDs were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. In short, 0.03 g of 4-ASA and 0.05 g of PEI were added into 22 mL of DI water, shaken and vibrated with ultrasonic apparatus for 10 min until dissolved. The solution was adjusted the pH value to 2.5 by adding HCl and mixed thoroughly for 3 min with N2. The mixture was shifted into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 3 h. The light brown solution was obtained. The prepared product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove the insoluble precipitation. To further obtain the clear liquid, 0.22 μM microporous filter was used to filtrate. Subsequently, the supernatant was treated with 4000 rpm for 30 min with an ultrafiltration tube (MWCO = 1000). The dark brown powder was obtained by freeze drying method. The yield of 4-ACDs obtained is 86.36 % ± 2.13 %. When studying the impacts of raw material on CDs, 4-ASA was replaced by SA, 3-ASA or 5-ASA, and the reaction conditions remain unchanged. After screening carbon sources, the fluorescence properties of CDs were optimized by changing other variables in the synthesis process, including pH, reaction temperature and time.

LZM was selected as the composite precursor. The protease and CDs were coupled through EDC-NHS method [23]. The steps are as follows: 50 mg of LZM was dissolved in 5 mL of DI water, and then 25 mg of EDC was added into the solution. The solution was stirred for 10 min, and then 20 mg of NHS was added when the solution was kept being stirred and activated for 30 min. 100 mg of 4-ACDs was dissolved in 11 mL of DI water, and then added into the previous solution, which was then kept stirred for 3 h. The solution was then transferred to an oil bath and stirred at 37 °C for 24 h. The CDs-LZM solution was treated with 4000 rpm for 5 min with an ultrafiltration tube (MWCO = 30,000). The supernatant solution obtained after ultrafiltration was lyophilized to obtain purified CDs-LZM brown powder.

Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis), Escherichia coli (E.coli) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) were cultured in MH broth medium. After overnight incubation at 37 °C at 180 rpm in shaking incubator, a first-generation activated bacterial suspension was obtained. The bacterial suspension was re-seeded into the fresh medium at 1:40 and continued overnight for the growth of bacterial cells to achieve log phase.

The MICs of 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM were studied by microdilution method with a 96-well plate. Briefly, the materials were formulated into solutions of different concentrations (50 μL) and inoculated with various types of bacterial suspensions (50 μL, 107 CFU mL−1) in 96-well plates, and then were placed at 37 °C for 12 h in the incubator. In order to obtain an intuitive and visualized phenomenon, after the incubation, 5 μL of 5 mg mL−1 MTT was added for 30 min in the dark, and then 100 μL of DMSO was transferred for colour rendering. The lowest concentration of the clear suspension was obtained as the MIC value of the sample against bacteria.

The medium for incubating biofilm was TSB medium (1 g L−1 glucose, 15 g L−1 trypticase soy broth and 2.5 g L−1 NaCl). The biofilms of S. aureus were selected as the research target, the inhibition curves of 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM on biofilms were investigated. The materials were prepared into different concentrations, and 50 μL of them were added to 96-well plate, then 50 μL of bacterial suspensions (5 × 107 CFU mL−1) were added, mixed well, and cultured at 37 °C for 48 h, and the OD600 in this period were recorded. The inhibition curves of OD600 as a function of time at different concentrations were drawn. The inhibition rate was obtained from the Eq. (1):
(1)
Inhibition rate
%
=
OD a
−
OD b
/
OD a
× 100 %
where OD a and OD b are the optical densities treated with the absence and presence of 4-ACDs, LZM or CDs-LZM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elimination of bacterial mature biofilm assay
ce:section.ce:para:
The ameliorative crystal violet (CV) method was applied to evaluate the elimination of mature biofilms indirectly [34]. The elimination rates of mature S. aureus biofilms by 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM were studied as follows. First, biofilms were cultured by adding 800 μL of bacterial suspensions (2 × 107 CFU mL−1, incubated in TSB medium) to 48-well plate and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h, mature biofilms can be obtained at the bottom of the plate through bacterial adhesion. Next, 500 μL of upper suspensions were slowly sucked, 300 μL of 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM of different concentrations were then added along the wall, and the suspensions were continued to incubate at 37 °C for 12 h. After the incubation, the culture medium and sample solutions were discarded, cleaned with sterile water three times, and dried. The biofilms were dyed with 0.05 % crystal violet for 20 min, washed to remove excess dye with DI water, dried, and then the crystal violets on the biofilms were dissolved in 250 μL of 30 % glacial acetic acid with shaking for 5 min. Finally, the OD600 was measured. All experiments were repeated 3 times. The elimination rate was obtained from the Eq. (2):
(2)
Elimination rate
%
=
OD c
−
OD s
/
OD c
× 100 %
where OD c and OD s are the optical densities treated without and with 4-ACDs, LZM or CDs-LZM, respectively.

The extraction and treatment process of EPS was referred to the reference [35] and modified. The EPS matrix was obtained by incubating the S. aureus biofilms. First, biofilms were cultured by adding 800 μL of bacterial suspensions (4 × 107 CFU mL−1, incubated in TSB medium) to 48-well plate at 37 °C for 12 h, and then the bacterial suspensions were continued to incubate at 37 °C for 12 h in the absence and presence of 4-ACDs (200 μg mL−1), LZM and CDs-LZM (both 60 μg mL−1). The culture medium and sample solutions were discarded, and cleaned with sterile water three times. Next, appropriate sterile PBS was added to collect EPS. The obtained EPS samples were treated by whirling for 20 min and vibrating with ultrasonic apparatus for 5 min.

Polysaccharides of EPS were measured by the modified sulfuric acid-phenol method [36]. 200 μL of EPS samples were added into 600 μL of 98 % concentrated H2SO4 and 200 μL of phenol, and mixed evenly, then the solutions were put under 50 °C for 30 min, finally the OD was measured at 490 nm.

Proteins of EPS were measured by Bicinchoninic Acid (BCA) method. Based on the instructions of the purchased BCA protein assay kit, 20 μL of EPS samples were added to a 96-well plate, and 200 μL of BCA working reagents were added (working fluid A: working fluid B = 50: 1, v/v). The solutions were set under 37 °C for 30 min, finally the OD was measured at 562 nm.

Nucleic acids of EPS were measured by modified diphenylamine colorimetry method according to reference [37]. First, diphenylamine reagent was prepared: 0.2 g of recrystallized diphenylamine was dissolved in 20 mL of acetic acid, then 550 μL of 98 % concentrated H2SO4 was added and mixed well. 200 μL of 1.6 % acetaldehyde solution was added before using. 450 μL of EPS samples were added into 900 μL of diphenylamine reagent, shaken well, and kept at 60 °C for 1 h. Finally, the OD was measured at 595 nm.

The reduction rates of polysaccharide, protein and nucleic acid were obtained from the Eq. (3):
(3)
Reduction rate
%
=
OD p
−
OD q
/
OD p
× 100 %
where OD p and OD q are the optical densities with the absence and presence of 4-ACDs, LZM or CDs-LZM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation of bacteria with the scanning electron microscope (SEM)
ce:section.ce:para: The impacts of 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM upon the bacterial morphology were evaluated through SEM. After culturing P. aeruginosa cells overnight, 1 mL of the suspension (2 aliquots) was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 3 min, the supernatants were removed, and the bacterial cells were re-suspended in 1 mL 4-ACDs or CDs-LZM solution (both 1 mg mL−1) for 5 h. The treated bacteria were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 3 min and washed with DI water 3 times. The collected bacteria were coated on suitable coverslips, and soaked in glutaraldehyde (2.5 %) overnight at 2–8 °C. The soaked bacterial samples were washed with DI water 3 times. Then, the bacterial cells were dehydrated with increasing concentrations of ethanol reagents (35 %, 50 %, 70 %, 80 % and 90 %) for 15 min. The samples were then soaked in 100 % ethanol for 30 min to complete the dehydration, and reagents were repeated twice. The dehydrated bacterial samples were treated by freeze-drying for 2.5 h. Before being tested, gold was sprayed on the coverslips, and imaged by SEM. The treatment steps of S. aureus are the same.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal laser microscopic (CLSM) imaging of bacteria and biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: S. aureus was taken as the test bacterium. The bacteria cells were cultured overnight. 2 mL bacterial suspension was added in 3 tubes respectively, centrifuged at 1800 rpm for 6 min, washed twice with 2 mL PBS solution (pH = 7.4) and the upper suspensions were removed. The obtained bacterial sediments were added into 1 mL PBS solution (blank control), 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM solution (both 200 μg mL−1) respectively. The solutions were set in the shaking incubator at 37 °C for 2.5 h. Finally, the treated cells were viewed under a 63× oil lens with a confocal fluorescence microscope.

The mature biofilms of S. aureus were cultured by adding 1 mL of bacterial suspensions (1 × 108 CFU mL−1, incubated in TSB medium) to glass-bottom cell culture dish at 37 °C for 12 h. Then, the upper suspensions were removed and 1 mL 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM solution (both 1 mg mL−1) were added respectively, and continued to incubate for 5 h. The culture medium and sample solutions were discarded, and cleaned with sterile water three times. Finally, the treated biofilms were observed under a 63× oil lens with a confocal fluorescence microscope.

The entire set of experiments was repeated thrice, the Waller Duncan's test with SPSS 22.0 were the methods employed to find the significance, and P < 0.05 was used to indicate the significant difference of one-way ANOVA. The results are shown as mean ± standard deviation.

Benzene ring-containing salicylic acid and its derivatives were selected as carbon sources and polyethylene imine as nitrogen source to prepare high-quality long-wavelength-emitting CDs through a hydrothermal route. To select the optimal carbon source, the impacts of the structure of raw materials (substitute type, position) upon the optical properties of the products have been investigated. Four kinds of salicylic acids and their derivatives (SA, 3-ASA, 4-ASA and 5-ASA) were selected to prepare CDs, and the products were named as SA-CDs, 3-ACDs, 4-ACDs and 5-ACDs respectively. As shown in the fluorescence spectra of these four CDs (Scheme 1 ), the presence of amino group on the benzene ring and its substitution position both play a critical role in the fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength of prepared CDs.

Firstly, when salicylic acid without amino substitution was selected as the carbon source, the fluorescence intensity of SA-CDs was incredibly weak with emission wavelength (450 nm) obviously shorter than other three samples, which might be attributed to the lack of active amino group on the benzene ring in SA structure, unbeneficial to the intermolecular conjugation polymerization process. Secondly, the emission wavelengths of other CDs prepared with amino-containing salicylic acid as carbon source exhibited obvious redshift (490 nm–525 nm) with fluorescence intensity difference, indicating that the amino group on the benzene ring, as the electron donor, is conducive to improve the conjugation of CDs and enhances the activity of electron cloud, thereby indirectly increasing the QY and facilitating the redshift of the absorption peak of CDs [38]. The emission wavelength of 4-ACDs is the longest (525 nm) among the three CDs, and its fluorescence intensity is 40 and 8 times stronger than those of 3-ACDs and 5-ACDs, which may be attributed to the close relationship between the position of amino groups on the parent ring and the steric hindrance effect of intermolecular polymerization. The amino group on the carboxyl para position has smaller steric hindrance, beneficial to the formation of carbon core, and the amino groups that do not participate in the polymerization reaction enhanced the conjugation of π electrons, conducive to the redshift of the fluorophores and the enhancement of fluorescence intensity. The amino group of 3-ASA is in the meta position of carboxyl group, and 3-ACDs exhibit the weakest fluorescence. Though the amino group in 5-ASA is also at the meta position of carboxyl group, it is in the para position with the hydroxyl group, so the relatively small steric hindrance effect is beneficial to the formation of strong fluorophores. In this paper, 4-ASA was selected as carbon source for further studies on the optimization of synthesis parameters, the filtrations and applications of CDs, expecting to provide reference for the synthesis of other high-quality carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impacts of pH value of precursor solution, reaction temperature and reaction time
ce:section.ce:para: The pH value of the solution, reaction temperature and time all have critical impacts on the fluorescence intensity of prepared 4-ACDs. The pH value of the precursor has almost no impact on the emission wavelength of the products, but exhibits a great effect on the fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. 1(a), the optimal pH value for the precursor solution is 2.5. With the increase of pH value, the fluorescence intensity of the product greatly decreases. It is believed that the carboxyl group on 4-ASA benzene ring plays important role in the synthesis process of CDs, and it exists as -COOH in acidic environment, which could dehydrate and bind with -NH2 in PEI, and also react with -NH2 in 4-ASA through intermolecular dehydration and condensation interaction, beneficial to the growth of conjugated systems and the formation of copolymers. In addition, lower pH value can increase the degree of carbonation of CDs, and H+ as a reaction mechanism can catalyze dehydration reaction [39]. However, in neutral and alkaline environments, the carboxyl group exists as ion state (-COO−), not conducive to the dehydration reaction, leading to reduced fluorescent products and lower fluorescence intensity.

Similarly, the impacts of different reaction temperatures and times were also investigated. As shown in Fig. 1(b) and (c), the optimal reaction temperature and time are 200 °C and 3 h respectively. Excessive reaction temperature and time would lead to hyper‑carbonization and thus the loss of active groups on the surface, while the low reaction temperature and shorter reaction time would make less fluorescent products during the formation of carbon core, leading to weakened fluorescence intensity [40].

4-ACDs (4-ASA as carbon source, pH 2.5, 200 °C and 3 h) were selected as the target for the experiments of characterization and antibacterial activity. The particle size and morphology of 4-ACDs are shown in Fig. 2(a), and the TEM image shows that 4-ACDs are in a spherical shape with well dispersity and size uniformity. The left inset shows that the size of 4-ACDs is normally distributed with average particle size at 2.5 nm, and the right inset is the HRTEM image of 4-ACDs, showing the clear lattice stripes with lattice spacing at 0.24 nm, which is attributed to the in-plane lattice of graphene (100) [41].

Fig. 2(b) shows the optical properties of 4-ACDs in the aqueous solution. It is yellow solution under the fluorescent lamp and exhibits strong yellow-green fluorescence under UV light (365 nm). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of 4-ACDs has two absorption peaks. The peak at 258 nm is attributed to the π → π* transition on the sp2 hybrid orbit (C=C) of the benzene ring on the surface of 4-ACDs, and the peak at 383 nm is attributed to the n → π* transition on C O/C=N groups produced by induced excited defected state on the surface of 4-ACDs [42]. As shown in fluorescence spectra, the optimal excitation wavelength of 4-ACDs is 390 nm, and the emission wavelength is 525 nm. The prepared 4-ACDs exhibit excitation-independent fluorescence behavior (Fig. 2(c)). When the excitation wavelength shifts from 350 nm to 450 nm, all the emission wavelengths are at 525 nm, which may be attributed to the surface states/defects emission of 4-ACDs [43,44]. Usually, the excitation-independent CDs save tedious multicolor separation steps, reduce production costs and are a promising nanomaterial in biology [45]. With rhodamine 6G (QY = 95 %, λex = 420 nm, ethanol as solvent) as reference, the relative quantum yield (QY) of 4-ACDs is 20.42 % when λex/λem is at 420/435–680 nm.

As shown in Fig. 2(d), under the illumination of UV light (365 nm), 4-ACDs exhibit different responses to pH values. The fluorescence intensity is strong in acidic environment and is obviously weak in neutral and alkaline environment. Under the excitation wavelength at 390 nm, the emission peak of 4-ACDs shows a slight blueshift with the increase of pH value. When the pH increases from 2 to 4, the fluorescence intensity slightly increases, while when pH reaches 5–7, the intensity greatly drops, and then stays stable in the range of 8–13. This phenomenon may be attributed to the protonation or deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of 4-ACDs [46].
The FT-IR spectra and XPS spectra results (in the supplementary materials) further prove the presence of amino, amide and phenolic hydroxyl functional groups of prepared 4-ACDs.

As known from the functional groups on the surface of 4-ACDs and their properties, the rich amino groups on the surface make them possible to prepare CDs-LZM through classical EDC-NHS coupling method while LZM contains carboxyl groups. The surface structure, particle size and surface charge of CDs-LZM complex have been characterized through a series of means. As shown from the HRTEM image (Fig. 3 ), compared with the particle size of 4-ACDs (2.5 nm), the size of CDs-LZM complex obviously increases (5.7 nm) with heterogeneous morphology. The lattice spacing of the complex is 0.31 nm, and it still possesses the lattice of graphene. The increased lattice and particle size might be attributed to the doping of C, N and O elements from LZM, preliminarily confirming the successful coupling between LZM and 4-ACDs [23].

UV–vis spectrology was used to study the absorption spectra of 4-ACDs, LZM and CD-LZM. As shown in Fig. 4(a), CDs-LZM complex retains the absorption peak of LZM at 280 nm, which is an obvious characteristic peak of protein [47]. Besides, CDs-LZM also has two obvious absorption peaks at 258 nm and 383 nm, corresponding to the absorption peaks of naked 4-ACDs, further proving the successful preparation of CDs-LZM. As shown in the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4(a)), the optimal excitation wavelength of CDs-LZM is at 390 nm with emission wavelength at 525 nm, which is in accordance with that of 4-ACDs and confirms that the complex maintains the preeminent optical properties from 4-ACDs.

FT-IR spectra were used to observe the secondary structure of CDs-LZM complex ((Fig. 4(b)). Compared with 4-ACDs, the band absorption at 3304 cm−1 is attributed to the bending transition of OH and NH, which is the clue of interaction between 4-ACDs and LZM through the amino group. The peak at 2964 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of CH, while the peak at 2928 cm−1 may be the absorption of LZM, proving the existence of LZM. It is known that LZM possesses three characteristic peaks in the mid-infrared range, which are corresponding to amide I (1700–1600 cm−1), amide II (1600–1500 cm−1) and amide III (1320–1230 cm−1) [48]. The peak at 1657 cm−1 in the spectrum of CDs-LZM is the axial stretching of the amide group CO, that is, the transfer of the amide I peak, while that peak at 1541 cm−1 is assigned to the bending vibration of NH and the stretching of CN, that is, the peak of amide II moves to a higher wavelength, which are corresponding to the characteristic absorption peaks of LZM. The FT-IR spectra show that the characteristic structures of LZM and 4-ACDs remain after their coupling.

Since the surface charge of the material set critical impact upon the antibacterial ability to the prepared product [49,50], Zeta potential was used to investigate the characteristics of LZM, 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM ((Fig. 4(c)). Under the same concentration, the Zeta potentials of LZM, 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM aqueous solutions are +20.1 mV, +51.8 mV and + 42.2 mV respectively, showing that the combination of 4-ACDs and LZM would change the surface charge of the complex, but the complex still possesses high positive-charge. The surface charge of the material can provide an idea for explaining the antibacterial effect in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The study on the antibacterial activities of 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM
The antibacterial activities of 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Table 1 , MIC was used to test the antibacterial activities of LZM, 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM with G+ bacteria (represented by S. aureus and B. subtilis) and G− bacteria (represented by E. coli and P. aeruginosa). The experiments show that 4-ACDs possess excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial activity with MIC values against S. aureus, E. coli and P. aeruginosa all under 100 μg mL−1. But its sensitivity to B. subtilis is low, and only when its concentration reaches 400 μg mL−1 can 4-ACDs exhibit antibacterial activity. We speculate this is because the complicated cell wall processing and the extremely long peptidoglycan chain of B. subtilis, and the cylinder on its inner surface that has a dense circumferential direction would strongly protect the integrity of bacterial cells [51,52], unbeneficial to the penetration of 4-ACDs into cells, and thus insensitive to the antibacterial agents.

Compared with 4-ACDs, CDs-LZM complex exhibited obviously stronger inhibiting ability against G+ bacteria, especially B. subtilis, but weaker ability against G− bacteria. The MIC value of CDs-LZM complex against S. aureus was 5 μg mL−1, 24 times higher than that against E. coli (120 μg mL−1). This result originates from the combined LZM on the surface of CDs-LZM, which possesses the specificity as protein against G+ bacteria, and the complex inherits such ability. LZM coming from egg albumin could catalyze the hydrolysis of β-1,4-glycosidic bond between N-acetylmuramic acid and N-acetylglucosamine, making cell membrane more vulnerable [26], speeding up the degrading of membrane structure and thus producing the antibacterial effect. LZM exhibits selective antibacterial activity with its MIC values to G+ bacteria (S. aureus and B. subtilis) at 5 and 6.25 μg mL−1, but without any antibacterial ability to G− bacteria, which is in accordance with the results of other references [48]. Therefore, CDs-LZM complex possesses a stronger and more sensitive inhibiting ability against B. subtilis. Obviously, the inhibiting ability of CDs-LZM against G+ bacteria is close to that of LZM, and the ability against G− bacteria is close to 4-ACDs, exhibiting broader antibacterial spectrum.

To investigate the antibacterial mechanism of 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM, S. aureus and P. aeruginosa were selected as the target bacteria of G+ and G− bacteria to observe the impacts of 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM upon the surface morphology of the bacteria. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the untreated S. aureus is in spherical shape, and the surface is full and complete. The normally developed and divided cells can also be observed. However, after the treatment of 4-ACDs or CDs-LZM (Fig. 5(b, c)), the cell surfaces show hollowness, and some even break up and collapse, with cell content exosmosis, showing that 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM kill bacteria by destroying their cell membranes. The nano-scaled particle size of 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM promotes easy penetration into cell membrane. Once the membrane as the barrier is damaged, the cell substances would leak to the outside environment, which destroys the normal physiological function of cells [9,53]. In addition, CDs-LZM retains the structural characteristic of LZM, which could make the cell membrane easier to be broken up and accelerate the degradation of cell membrane structure with the ability to hydrolyze polysaccharides. This is the reason why the damage caused by CDs-LZM to S. aureus cell membrane is more serious than that caused by 4-ACDs, and also one of the reasons that the antibacterial activity of CDs-LZM complex is stronger than that of 4-ACDs.

It is well known that the structural difference between G+ and G− bacteria is the major reason for their different sensitivity to antibacterial agents [9,54]. The cell wall of G− bacteria contains thin peptidoglycan layer, lipopolysaccharide layer and phospholipid layer, and the asymmetric bilayer structure of lipopolysaccharide and phospholipid form the defensive outer membrane (embedded with specific porins and substrate channels) [54,55]. As shown in Fig. 5(d-f), the normal P. aeruginosa is rod-shaped with a slightly rough surface. After being treated by 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM, the surface of bacteria becomes rougher or even damaged. It is speculated that 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM possess excellent hydrophilicity, which could help them enter the membrane through porin, and realize electrostatic absorption onto the cell wall of P. aeruginosa peptidoglycan, and thus destroy cell structure to produce antibacterial effect. Because G− bacteria have more complicated bacteria surface structures than G+ bacteria, the inhibiting activities of 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM against G− bacteria are much weaker.

Circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy is an effective device to detect the secondary structure of DNA and proteins in solution [56,57]. The difference between 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM in antibacterial activity against the DNA and proteins of S. aureus has been investigated. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the characteristic CD peaks of most nucleotides are in the range of 220–280 nm, depending on the components of base pairs and their secondary structure. Compared with the control group, the group peaks of DNA of S. aureus treated by 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM are significantly reduced, showing the loosened double helix structure of DNA caused by 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM [23], and 4-ACDs make more serious damage to the DNA of S. aureus. After penetrating the cell membrane of bacteria, 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM could well spread inside bacteria because of their nano-scaled particle size, and then have easily contacted with the naked bacterial DNA, loosening its structure, and further inhibiting the reproduction of the bacteria [58].

The interactions between total proteins of S. aureus and 4-ACDs/CDs-LZM have been studied through CD spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the single S. aureus proteins exhibit broad absorption peak at 225 nm due to the π → π* transition of α-helical structure [23]. After being dealt with 4-ACDs, the peaks of protein do not show obvious change, but after being treated by CDs-LZM complex, there are four protein absorption peaks at 207, 211, 216 and 222 nm, with weakened CD signals, showing the presence of CDs-LZM could impact the structural characteristic of proteins. This is also one of the major reasons that the antibacterial activity of CDs-LZM is stronger than that of 4-ACDs.

The above experiments show that 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM not only destroy the integrity of cell membrane of bacteria, but also interact with genetic material inside the bacteria, causing function disorder, hindering bacterial growth and reproduction and finally leading to the death of bacteria. Their differences in the impacts upon cell membrane, DNA and proteins are in accordance to their difference in antibacterial activities. The proposed antibacterial mechanism laid a foundation for the subsequent antibiofilm behavior.

The superb biocompatibility is the critical requirement for fluorescent nanomaterials before their applications in biological imaging [5]. As shown in Fig. 7 , 4-ACDs show almost no impact upon J774A.1 macrophages when its concentration is under 100 μg mL−1. However, when concentration rises to 200 μg mL−1, the survival rate of bacteria drops to 62 % while that treated by CDs-LZM still remains above 75 %, showing the combination of 4-ACDs and LZM could obviously improve the biocompatibility of 4-ACDs. Considering MICs of both materials are lower than 200 μg mL−1 when used against pathogenic bacteria purpose, the prepared 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM both could be applied in biomedical fields.

The excellent optical properties of 4-ACDs (yellow-green-fluorescent, high QY and great fluorescence stability, etc.) have been inherited to CDs-LZM complex. The super-small nano-scaled particle size and excellent biocompatibility of both materials ensure them ideal candidates as fluorescent probes in bacterial imaging. S. aureus was selected as the target bacterial model to test the bacteria labeling abilities of 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM. The treated S. aureus was set under CLSM to be observed (Fig. 8 ). Under different exciting wavelengths (493 nm, 557 nm), both 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM could still maintain the excellent fluorescent property after entering into bacteria, further confirming their good biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The antibiofilm activity of 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial biofilms play a protective role for bacteria from the damage of antibacterial agents [12], so the ability in inhibiting the growth of bacteria and the formation of biofilm are two ways to judge the antibacterial activity of the material. The effective elimination of biofilm is still a great challenge in the treatment of pathogenic infectious diseases [59]. Since S. aureus is the bacteria that widely exists in nature and causes biofilm infections in clinics [60], it was thus selected as the target bacteria for biofilm experiments, to assess the inhibiting and eliminating abilities of 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM. The abilities of these three materials to inhibit the growth of biofilm were observed, that is, adding 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM of different concentrations into bacterial suspension (2.5 × 107 CFU mL−1) in a 96 well-plate for 48 h to acquire the curve of biofilm inhibition degree versus time by measuring OD600. As shown in Fig. 9 , obviously, single 4-ACDs could not effectively inhibit the formation of biofilm. With the increase of time, the inhibition capacity of 4-ACDs showed a downward trend, and the inhibition rate was still lower than 15 % at a high concentration (400 μg mL−1) after 48 h, showing bacterial biofilm colonization could not be inhibited. However, compared with biofilm of the control group, the inhibition rate of CDs-LZM (60 μg mL−1) still reached 86 % after 48 h, and the inhibition efficiency was significant, equivalent to that of LZM (86 %) at the same concentration. These results show that the CDs-LZM complex exhibits obvious antibacterial ability and the ability to inhibit the growth of biofilm, with certain potentials to eliminate biofilm.

The abilities of 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM in eliminating the mature biofilm of S. aureus were then investigated. After culturing S. aureus in a 48 well-plate to obtain stable biofilms, 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM were co-incubated with the biofilms for 12 h. Fig. 10 shows the change of biomass in biofilm after CV dyeing. The elimination abilities of three materials on mature biofilms of S. aureus were acquired by measuring OD600. The elimination rates have certain concentration dependence, and ascend with the increase of concentration. The results show that the elimination ability of 4-ACDs is the worst, with elimination rate only at 15.4 % at 400 μg mL−1, showing that 4-ACDs could not effectively destroy the mature biofilm of S. aureus. The damage of LZM to mature biofilm showed a moderate impact, with the elimination rate at 64 % at 120 μg mL−1. CDs-LZM complex exhibits the more efficient eliminating ability to mature biofilm with elimination rate reaching 79 % at 60 μg mL−1. Moreover, in the orifice plate diagram after CV staining, it can be observed with the naked eye that the biofilms became significantly less, thinner with reduced adhesion ability to the pore wall, and showed loose shape, further proving the effective eliminating ability prepared CDs-LZM complex against mature biofilm.

To understand the metabolic activity of mature biofilm after being destroyed, the MTT assay was used indirectly to test the viability of bacterial cells remaining in biofilm [61]. As shown in Fig. 11 , the OD values of biofilm of S. aureus treated by 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM of various concentrations show that the viabilities of bacteria are slightly dependent on the concentration of the materials, which descend with the increase of concentration. The experiment results show that 4-ACDs exhibited the weakest inactivation capacity to the bacteria inside biofilm. The cell activity is still >50 % when reaches 400 μg mL−1, illustrating its inability to effectively inhibit the proliferation of bacteria and the formation and destruction of biofilm, which is in accordance to the results of previous experiments. LZM and CDs-LZM could obviously reduce the bacteria activity in the biofilm, and CDs-LZM exhibited the best inhibiting ability. The bacteria activity could be reduced to lower than 15 % when the concentration of CDs-LZM is only 60 μg mL−1, and the biological metabolic activity is effectively inhibited. This result is also consistent with the determination results of biofilm elimination experiments. Therefore, the antibiofilm platform based on CDs-LZM exhibits certain application potentials.

Relevant researches showed that polysaccharides, proteins, nucleic acids and other substances in EPS not only maintain the morphology and structure of biofilm, but also provide benefits for the adhesion, proliferation and protection for the bacteria, and maintain the survival of microorganisms in the biofilm, thus enhancing the pathogenicity and resistance against antibacterial agents [8,12,62]. To understand the antibiofilm mechanism, the impacts of 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM upon EPS in the biofilm of S. aureus have been investigated from three aspects: polysaccharide, protein and nucleic acid. As shown in Fig. 12 , the quantitative measurements of polysaccharides, proteins, nucleic acid in EPS treated with 4-ACDs, LZM and CDs-LZM, and the control groups show that the contents of the three substances in treated EPS all reduced to varying degrees and polysaccharides were dominant compared with the contents of proteins and nucleic acids. Compared with EPS in control group, the contents of polysaccharides, proteins, nucleic acid of EPS treated by 4-ACDs reduced 32 %, 31.8 % and 29.8 % respectively, and the reduction rates of these three substances are close. However, the contents of the substances in EPS treated by LZM reduced 42 %, 18.7 % and 34.2 % respectively, indicating the difference of LZM in interacting with different components of biofilm, related to the hydrolyzing ability of polysaccharide from LZM. The reduction rate of the contents of EPS components treated by CDs-LZM is the greatest, at 53.9 %, 40.7 % and 45.5 % respectively, showing that the content of polysaccharide is mostly affected by CDs-LZM, while the content of protein and nucleic acid decreased to a similar extent, both >40 %. The experiment results show that during the process of decomposing the EPS matrix, CDs-LZM exhibits superiority over the other two materials, which is in accordance to the biomass results of destroying mature biofilm. It was thus speculated that CDs-LZM realizes its inhibition and elimination effects against the mature biofilm of S. aureus through interrupting synthesis and expression of polysaccharides, extracellular proteins and nucleic acids.

Fluorescent imaging could monitor bacterial activity, and the effects of inhibition and elimination of biofilm [63]. Since CDs-LZM can effectively eliminate the biofilm of S. aureus, imaging could be used to study the effects of the complex against biofilm. 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM were added into cultured S. aureus biofilm in confocal dish for 5 h. The visualization function of CLSM shows that 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM could well penetrate into the compact and clustered biofilms (Fig. 13 ). The thickness of 4-ACDs-treated biofilm is 10 μm, and the distribution of bacteria is still dense, while the biofilm treated by CDs-LZM is no longer dense, the biomass is reduced, and the thickness of biofilm greatly decreased, indicating CDs-LZM exhibits obvious effect against the complex dynamic structure of S. aureus biofilm, and could invade into the biofilm barrier and kill the adherent bacteria inside to prevent the proliferation and secretion of the bacteria, finally realizing the effect of elimination. The fluorescent imaging of bacteria could realize traceable research on the spatial-temporal dynamics and inhibiting mechanisms.

This paper reports the synthesis of long-wavelength emitting 4-ACDs with excellent optical properties and QYs at 20.42 %, by screening synthetic materials and using 4-ASA and PEI as raw materials (200 °C, 3 h), and the product has been characterized by multiple means. The antibacterial experiments results show that 4-ACDs exhibit broad-spectrum inhibiting ability against G+ and G− bacteria, but their ability against biofilm activity of S. aureus is still quite weak. Based on the excellent optical properties and potentials in biomedical applications, 4-ACDs were coupled with LZM to form CDs-LZM complex, and the complex showed great improvement against the activity of planktonic bacteria, especially against S. aureus and B. subtilis with MICs at 5 and 3.125 μg mL−1. The prepared 4-ACDs and CDs-LZM possess excellent biocompatibility and good combining ability with bacterial cells, and have been successfully applied in the imaging of S. aureus. Furthermore, CDs-LZM can effectively inhibit the formation of S. aureus biofilm, and destroy its mature biofilm and reduce biomass. The research shows that compared with 4-ACDs and LZM, CDs-LZM can eliminate the activity of bacterial cells in biofilm, and decompose the extracellular matrix (polysaccharide, protein and nucleic acid) of mature biofilm, degrade the proliferation and differentiation of bacteria, and finally realize the visual monitoring on the impacts on the biofilm thickness. CDs-LZM could be used as an effective antibacterial and antibiofilm candidate in treating antibiotic-resistant biofilm infections. Although CDs-LZM has a significant elimination effect on S. aureus biofilm due to its inheritance from characteristics of LZM and is only more sensitive to G+ bacteria, it still has some limits on the biofilm of G− bacteria. Therefore, the elimination of bacterial biofilm still needs further exploration.

Dan Zhao: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoyun Li: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Mengyu Xu: Investigation. Yan Jiao: Investigation. Huan Liu: Investigation. Xincai Xiao: Conceptualization, Validation. Haiyan Zhao: Conceptualization, Validation.
